CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized with a clear progression from motivation to method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (a fully non-monotonic Covington system and an approximate dynamic oracle). Notation and definitions appear consistent and the writing quality is good, with effective figures/tables and examples supporting the exposition. A few presentation gaps remain for reproducibility-focused readers: hyperparameters and code availability are not reported, and the description of the approximate loss terms and the computational caveats (e.g., cycle computations, 100,000-transition limit) could be explained more accessibly. Overall the paper is well-written with minor presentation issues that, if addressed, would make replication easier. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from introduction through method, experiments, and conclusion, and it states the main contributions early (a fully non-monotonic Covington system and an approximate dynamic oracle). Notation and formal framing seem consistent and the writing quality is reported as good, with adequate related-work coverage and informative experimental comparisons. Minor presentation issues remain: the dynamic-oracle approximation and its computational limitations could use clearer demarcation (e.g. exact vs. approximate loss trade-offs), and practical details such as hyperparameters and code availability are not reported, which would aid reproducibility.

A few presentation gaps remain for reproducibility-focused readers: hyperparameters and code availability are not reported, and the description of the approximate loss terms and the computational caveats (e.g., cycle computations, 100,000-transition limit) could be explained more accessibly. Overall the paper is well-written with minor presentation issues that, if addressed, would make replication easier.

Minor presentation issues remain: the dynamic-oracle approximation and its computational limitations could use clearer demarcation (e.g. exact vs. approximate loss trade-offs), and practical details such as hyperparameters and code availability are not reported, which would aid reproducibility.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a clear, non-trivial extension of transition-based parsing by making the Covington non-projective system fully non-monotonic (allowing arbitrary arc replacement via deletion to preserve single-head and acyclicity) and by designing an efficient approximate dynamic oracle for this harder setting. This appears to be the first parser combining full non-monotonicity with support for non-projective dependency structures, and the authors explicitly compare to the closest monotonic baseline (Gómez-Rodríguez & Fernández-González 2015). From my reproducible-systems perspective the idea is novel and well-differentiated from prior work, though the need for approximate loss and reported computational limitations indicate the advance is notable rather than a radical departure. | B(4): The paper presents a notable and non-trivial novelty: a fully non-monotonic transition system applied to the non-projective Covington parser that permits arbitrary arc replacement by deleting offending arcs, which—according to the summary—is the first non-monotonic parser able to produce non-projective analyses. The introduction of an efficient approximate dynamic oracle for this challenging non-monotonic, non-projective setting is also an original methodological contribution beyond prior monotonic or projective-focused work. While this builds on established transition-parsing machinery, the combination and the technical handling of single-head and acyclicity via deletions represent a clear differentiation from prior work and would inform experts, though it stops short of a breakthrough theoretical guarantee (hence not a top score).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a clear, non-trivial extension of transition-based parsing by making the Covington non-projective system fully non-monotonic (allowing arbitrary arc replacement via deletion to preserve single-head and acyclicity) and by designing an efficient approximate dynamic oracle for this harder setting. This appears to be the first parser combining full non-monotonicity with support for non-projective dependency structures, and the authors explicitly compare to the closest monotonic baseline (Gómez-Rodríguez & Fernández-González 2015). From my reproducible-systems perspective the idea is novel and well-differentiated from prior work, though the need for approximate loss and reported computational limitations indicate the advance is notable rather than a radical departure.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable and non-trivial novelty: a fully non-monotonic transition system applied to the non-projective Covington parser that permits arbitrary arc replacement by deleting offending arcs, which—according to the summary—is the first non-monotonic parser able to produce non-projective analyses. The introduction of an efficient approximate dynamic oracle for this challenging non-monotonic, non-projective setting is also an original methodological contribution beyond prior monotonic or projective-focused work. While this builds on established transition-parsing machinery, the combination and the technical handling of single-head and acyclicity via deletions represent a clear differentiation from prior work and would inform experts, though it stops short of a breakthrough theoretical guarantee (hence not a top score).

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines: static and dynamic monotonic oracles and the monotonic Covington parser of Gómez-Rodríguez & Fernández-González (2015), evaluated across many CoNLL-X/XI languages using the same accuracy metric. Comparisons appear largely fair (same datasets/metrics), but some experimental limitations—use of an approximate oracle, restriction to the first 100k transitions for parts of the analysis, and lack of reported hyperparameters or code—limit reproducibility and make it harder to fully assess parity of conditions. The authors acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably and discuss algorithmic differences, but missing implementation/runtime details and full exact-loss comparisons are notable omissions. | B(4): The paper evaluates against appropriate baselines (static oracle, dynamic monotonic oracle, and the prior monotonic Covington parser of Gómez-Rodríguez & Fernández-González 2015) across many CoNLL-X / Xl datasets using the same data and accuracy metrics, which is strong. Comparisons appear fair and the authors report majority-language gains for their non-monotonic system; related work coverage is described as adequate. However, the manuscript relies on an approximate oracle (with some expensive exact terms left out and limits on long runs) and does not report hyperparameter/code details, and it is not explicit whether they acknowledge cases where prior methods match or outperform theirs—hence a minor deduction for incomplete transparency and potential omission of some comparative nuance.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines: static and dynamic monotonic oracles and the monotonic Covington parser of Gómez-Rodríguez & Fernández-González (2015), evaluated across many CoNLL-X/XI languages using the same accuracy metric. Comparisons appear largely fair (same datasets/metrics), but some experimental limitations—use of an approximate oracle, restriction to the first 100k transitions for parts of the analysis, and lack of reported hyperparameters or code—limit reproducibility and make it harder to fully assess parity of conditions. The authors acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably and discuss algorithmic differences, but missing implementation/runtime details and full exact-loss comparisons are notable omissions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates against appropriate baselines (static oracle, dynamic monotonic oracle, and the prior monotonic Covington parser of Gómez-Rodríguez & Fernández-González 2015) across many CoNLL-X / Xl datasets using the same data and accuracy metrics, which is strong. Comparisons appear fair and the authors report majority-language gains for their non-monotonic system; related work coverage is described as adequate. However, the manuscript relies on an approximate oracle (with some expensive exact terms left out and limits on long runs) and does not report hyperparameter/code details, and it is not explicit whether they acknowledge cases where prior methods match or outperform theirs—hence a minor deduction for incomplete transparency and potential omission of some comparative nuance.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper clearly states and supports its main algorithmic claims with derivations and empirical comparisons showing the non-monotonic parser often outperforms a monotonic baseline; the use of an approximate dynamic oracle is plausibly justified given stated computational hardness. However, important reproducibility and methodological details are missing in the summary: hyperparameters are not reported, code is not mentioned, experiments were truncated (first 100k transitions for some analyses), and no statistical testing is reported, which weakens confidence in the empirical claims. The mathematical approach appears reasonable but the reliance on approximations and omission of exact-loss computation details leaves open potential gaps in correctness guarantees. Overall the contribution seems promising but some experimental and reproducibility deficiencies prevent a higher score. | B(4): The paper provides a coherent technical proposal (a Covington-based transition system that enforces single-head and acyclicity by deleting offending arcs) and supports its empirical claim that the non-monotonic system outperforms monotonic baselines on the tested datasets. The authors are transparent about the approximate nature of the dynamic oracle and the computational difficulty of exact loss (e.g., expensive cycle computations), which explains the use of efficient loss approximations; this is a minor technical limitation rather than a fatal flaw. However, there are some gaps: no mention of full mathematical guarantees about the oracle’s optimality or formal bounds on approximation error, limited experimental detail (no reported hyperparameters or statistical testing) and the experimental limitation to the first 100,000 transitions for some analyses. Overall these are resolvable issues that do not overturn the core technical contribution.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states and supports its main algorithmic claims with derivations and empirical comparisons showing the non-monotonic parser often outperforms a monotonic baseline; the use of an approximate dynamic oracle is plausibly justified given stated computational hardness. However, important reproducibility and methodological details are missing in the summary: hyperparameters are not reported, code is not mentioned, experiments were truncated (first 100k transitions for some analyses), and no statistical testing is reported, which weakens confidence in the empirical claims. The mathematical approach appears reasonable but the reliance on approximations and omission of exact-loss computation details leaves open potential gaps in correctness guarantees. Overall the contribution seems promising but some experimental and reproducibility deficiencies prevent a higher score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides a coherent technical proposal (a Covington-based transition system that enforces single-head and acyclicity by deleting offending arcs) and supports its empirical claim that the non-monotonic system outperforms monotonic baselines on the tested datasets. The authors are transparent about the approximate nature of the dynamic oracle and the computational difficulty of exact loss (e.g., expensive cycle computations), which explains the use of efficient loss approximations; this is a minor technical limitation rather than a fatal flaw. However, there are some gaps: no mention of full mathematical guarantees about the oracle’s optimality or formal bounds on approximation error, limited experimental detail (no reported hyperparameters or statistical testing) and the experimental limitation to the first 100,000 transitions for some analyses. Overall these are resolvable issues that do not overturn the core technical contribution.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper evaluates its non-monotonic Covington parser across a broad set of 19 languages from CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI and compares against static, dynamic-monotonic, and the prior monotonic non-projective system, which provides reasonable empirical support for the main claim. However, important reproducibility and diagnostic elements are missing: hyperparameters, random seeds, code availability, and runtime/memory costs are not reported, and the dynamic oracle is only approximate without thorough ablations isolating which approximations drive gains. The authors do discuss the limitation that exact loss computation is expensive and that some analyses were curtailed to the first 100,000 transitions, but sensitivity analyses and more detailed failure-mode diagnostics are lacking, so the experimental depth is adequate but incomplete. | B(3): The empirical evaluation covers a wide range of languages (CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI datasets) and compares against relevant baselines (static and monotonic dynamic oracles and the Gómez-Rodríguez & Fernández-González monotonic system), supporting the core claim that non-monotonicity helps in many cases. However, key reproducibility details are missing (no code mention, hyperparameters not reported), and there are no explicit ablations or sensitivity analyses probing the impact of the approximate oracle choices versus exact loss computation. The paper does acknowledge limitations of the approximate oracle and computational constraints (e.g. analyses truncated to the first 100,000 transitions), but deeper failure-case analysis and more complete experimental controls are lacking.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates its non-monotonic Covington parser across a broad set of 19 languages from CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI and compares against static, dynamic-monotonic, and the prior monotonic non-projective system, which provides reasonable empirical support for the main claim. However, important reproducibility and diagnostic elements are missing: hyperparameters, random seeds, code availability, and runtime/memory costs are not reported, and the dynamic oracle is only approximate without thorough ablations isolating which approximations drive gains. The authors do discuss the limitation that exact loss computation is expensive and that some analyses were curtailed to the first 100,000 transitions, but sensitivity analyses and more detailed failure-mode diagnostics are lacking, so the experimental depth is adequate but incomplete.

reviewer_b.justification: The empirical evaluation covers a wide range of languages (CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI datasets) and compares against relevant baselines (static and monotonic dynamic oracles and the Gómez-Rodríguez & Fernández-González monotonic system), supporting the core claim that non-monotonicity helps in many cases. However, key reproducibility details are missing (no code mention, hyperparameters not reported), and there are no explicit ablations or sensitivity analyses probing the impact of the approximate oracle choices versus exact loss computation. The paper does acknowledge limitations of the approximate oracle and computational constraints (e.g. analyses truncated to the first 100,000 transitions), but deeper failure-case analysis and more complete experimental controls are lacking.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper tackles the important problem of extending non-monotonic transition parsing to the non-projective setting (Covington), and claims the first such parser with an approximate dynamic oracle — a concrete algorithmic advance that empirically outperforms monotonic baselines on most of 19 CoNLL languages. From a reproducibility and practitioner standpoint I note limitations that temper impact: the oracle is approximate (exact loss is computationally hard), some analyses were capped at 100k transitions, and key experimental details (code, hyperparameters, seeds) are not reported, which will slow adoption. Nonetheless, enabling arbitrary arc replacement for non-projective parsing is a meaningful contribution that should open new research directions and be adopted by researchers refining oracle approximations and implementations. | B(4): This paper addresses an important, longstanding challenge—combining full non-monotonicity with unrestricted non-projective transition parsing—by extending the Covington system to allow arbitrary arc replacement while maintaining single-head and acyclicity, and by proposing an efficient approximate dynamic oracle. The empirical finding that the non-monotonic parser outperforms the monotonic version on most of nineteen CoNLL-X/XI datasets suggests practitioners and researchers should reconsider monotonic assumptions in transition-based non-projective parsing. The approximate oracle and the practical engineering choices open clear new directions (better loss computation, exact/non-approximate oracles, and integration with global or neural training), so the contribution is likely to be built upon across languages, though its impact is tempered by reliance on approximations and greedy training rather than guaranteed exact inference.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles the important problem of extending non-monotonic transition parsing to the non-projective setting (Covington), and claims the first such parser with an approximate dynamic oracle — a concrete algorithmic advance that empirically outperforms monotonic baselines on most of 19 CoNLL languages. From a reproducibility and practitioner standpoint I note limitations that temper impact: the oracle is approximate (exact loss is computationally hard), some analyses were capped at 100k transitions, and key experimental details (code, hyperparameters, seeds) are not reported, which will slow adoption. Nonetheless, enabling arbitrary arc replacement for non-projective parsing is a meaningful contribution that should open new research directions and be adopted by researchers refining oracle approximations and implementations.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important, longstanding challenge—combining full non-monotonicity with unrestricted non-projective transition parsing—by extending the Covington system to allow arbitrary arc replacement while maintaining single-head and acyclicity, and by proposing an efficient approximate dynamic oracle. The empirical finding that the non-monotonic parser outperforms the monotonic version on most of nineteen CoNLL-X/XI datasets suggests practitioners and researchers should reconsider monotonic assumptions in transition-based non-projective parsing. The approximate oracle and the practical engineering choices open clear new directions (better loss computation, exact/non-approximate oracles, and integration with global or neural training), so the contribution is likely to be built upon across languages, though its impact is tempered by reliance on approximations and greedy training rather than guaranteed exact inference.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses dependency parsing — a core NLP topic — and targets the transition-based parsing community by extending Covington to fully non-monotonic, non-projective parsing with an approximate dynamic oracle; that makes it broadly within scope for a top NLP/ML venue. Methods and evaluation (comparisons to static and monotonic dynamic oracles across CoNLL-X/XI datasets and languages) align with expectations, and the work will interest the venue’s audience. My main concerns from a reproducibility standpoint (relevant for venue fit) are the lack of reported hyperparameters, no mention of released code, and limits on some analyses due to expensive exact-loss computations; these are minor scope issues but important for acceptance at a top conference. | B(5): This paper addresses a core parsing problem (non-projective dependency parsing) and introduces a novel full non-monotonic transition system plus an approximate dynamic oracle — topics squarely within top NLP/ML conference interests. The methodology (modifying the non-projective Covington system, approximate oracle design) and evaluation on CoNLL-X/XI multilingual benchmarks against relevant monotonic baselines match the community’s methodological and experimental expectations. The framing and datasets target the parsing and multilingual NLP audience appropriately, and the claimed empirical gains on multiple languages make it highly relevant to the venue.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses dependency parsing — a core NLP topic — and targets the transition-based parsing community by extending Covington to fully non-monotonic, non-projective parsing with an approximate dynamic oracle; that makes it broadly within scope for a top NLP/ML venue. Methods and evaluation (comparisons to static and monotonic dynamic oracles across CoNLL-X/XI datasets and languages) align with expectations, and the work will interest the venue’s audience. My main concerns from a reproducibility standpoint (relevant for venue fit) are the lack of reported hyperparameters, no mention of released code, and limits on some analyses due to expensive exact-loss computations; these are minor scope issues but important for acceptance at a top conference.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core parsing problem (non-projective dependency parsing) and introduces a novel full non-monotonic transition system plus an approximate dynamic oracle — topics squarely within top NLP/ML conference interests. The methodology (modifying the non-projective Covington system, approximate oracle design) and evaluation on CoNLL-X/XI multilingual benchmarks against relevant monotonic baselines match the community’s methodological and experimental expectations. The framing and datasets target the parsing and multilingual NLP audience appropriately, and the claimed empirical gains on multiple languages make it highly relevant to the venue.

main_claims: Introduces a fully non-monotonic transition system for the non-projective Covington parser that allows arbitrary arc replacements by deleting offending previous arcs to preserve single-head and acyclicity constraints.
Presents an approximate dynamic oracle for this non-monotonic, non-projective parser based on efficiently computable loss approximations.

Claims this is the first non-monotonic parser able to produce non-projective dependency analyses and shows experimentally that the non-monotonic oracle/parser outperforms the monotonic version on a majority of tested datasets.
_summary.methodology: Modify the Covington non-projective transition system so Right-Arc and Left-Arc always permitted and enforce single-head and acyclicity by deleting previous arcs when necessary; train greedily with an approximate dynamic oracle that uses efficient approximations of loss due to exact loss being computationally hard.
datasets: nineteen languages available from CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI (analysis limited to first 100,000 transitions per dataset)
baselines: monotonic version by Gómez-Rodríguez and Fernández-González (2015)
limitations: The dynamic oracle is approximate because computing the exact loss in the non-monotonic non-projective scenario is complex and inefficient.
Exact computation of certain non-monotonic terms requires expensive algorithms (e.g., finding all elementary cycles), so some analyses were limited to the first 100,000 transitions per dataset.

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Systems Analyst
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a decade of experience building and evaluating transition-based parsing systems and production NLP pipelines; has led reproducibility audits and implemented parsing models in research and industry settings.
traits: checks for thorough experimental controls: clear baselines, hyperparameter tuning procedures, and statistical significance
values reproducibility: requests code, seed management, dataset splits, and runtime/complexity measurements
looks for ablations and diagnostic experiments that isolate the contribution of the algorithmic change
examines implementation realism: greedy vs. beam decoding, training regimes (static vs. dynamic oracles), and runtime/memory trade-offs
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Practical, detail-oriented reviewer who focuses on experimental rigor, reproducibility, and whether the reported gains are well-supported by controlled comparisons and diagnostics.

reviewer_b.name: Parsing Methods & Impact Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Academic researcher specializing in dependency parsing and syntactic formalisms, with extensive publication record on non-projective parsing, transition and graph-based approaches, and oracle design.
traits: assesses novelty relative to the parsing literature and prior non-monotonic or non-projective methods
values theoretical clarity: precise definitions, guarantees (or lack thereof), and limitations of the proposed system
considers broader impact: applicability across languages, practical constraints, and how this advances the field's capabilities
notices connections to related paradigms (graph-based parsers, constraint-solving, and global inference) and expects discussion of trade-offs
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Context-aware reviewer who evaluates the paper's novelty, theoretical framing, and potential significance to parsing research and multilingual applications.
